name: d'Este Tarocchi — The Ferrarese Fragment (c. 1450)
description:
# d'Este (Estensi) Tarocchi

## At a glance

Among the **oldest surviving tarot of all** — sixteen hand-painted cards made in **Ferrara around 1450**, in the orbit of the ruling **House of Este**, contemporaries of the Milanese Visconti decks but from the *other* great early tarot court. Only **16 cards survive** (8 trumps + 8 court cards) of what was once a full pack; they are held in the **Cary Collection of Playing Cards at Yale's Beinecke Library** (PLAYING CARDS GEN 966) and shown here from Yale's open-access high-resolution scans.

## Why it matters — the B-order home

Ferrara is the home of the **B-order** trump sequence (the Eastern branch in Dummett's A/B/C scheme). Where Milan's Visconti decks head the **C-order** line that became the Tarot de Marseille, the d'Este cards are a rare, luxurious witness to Ferrara's parallel tradition — proof that tarot's invention was not a single Milanese event but a courtly fashion shared across northern Italy in the 1440s–50s.

## What survives

- **8 trumps:** Il Bagatto (the Magician), Il Papa (the Pope), Temperance, the Star, the Moon, the Sun, the World, and Il Matto (the Fool).
- **8 court cards:** kings, queens, a cavalier and a jack across Swords, Batons, Coins and Cups.
- Card identifications are **Yale's own catalogue labels** — not guessed from iconography. The cards are unnumbered (as 15th-century hand-painted trumps were).

## Game, not divination

Like the Visconti decks, this is a **luxury court game** object — *trionfi*, three centuries before any divinatory use. No cartomantic meaning is native to it.

## Provenance & evidence

Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale — Cary Collection (Mary Flagler Cary, 1967); PLAYING CARDS GEN 966; cards 140 × 78 mm; completely digitized, open access (credit: Yale University Library). Dating ('c. 1450') and the Ferrarese/Este attribution follow the standard scholarship but, as with all the early hand-painted decks, remain debated.

## Sources

Yale Beinecke / Cary Collection catalogue; Michael Dummett, *The Game of Tarot* (1980); Thierry Depaulis on the Ferrarese decks.

---
*AI-assisted first-pass: high-resolution faces from Yale's open-access IIIF, named from Yale's catalogue. Corrections welcome.*

**RESEARCH NOTE (2026-06-13).** Dating is disputed: c. 1450 (used here) vs c. 1470s (Pollett). The deck's original composition is unknown. See research/decks/este-tarot.md.
_source: Yale Beinecke / Cary Collection of Playing Cards (PLAYING CARDS GEN 966), open access. Credit: Yale University Library.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.Scene: A crowned king in armour sits on a throne before a golden damask background. He wears a black brimmed hat over his crown and holds a large drawn sword diagonally across his body. Red and gold armour with elaborate tooling. A shield leans against the throne at lower right.
sections.Symbol: The sovereign intellect; the king who commands with the clarity and authority of the sword suit.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.Scene: A crowned queen in a richly decorated dark green and gold gown is seated on a throne. She holds what appears to be a sword in her left hand and a small object in her right. A shield bearing an eagle emblem stands behind her at upper right. Her curled hair flows below the crown.
sections.Symbol: Queenly authority paired with the sword of discernment; intelligence expressed through governance.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.Scene: An armoured knight on horseback charges to the right. He raises a sword overhead in his right hand while his left holds a shield. A defeated figure — crouching or fallen — lies beneath the horse's hooves. The gold damask background fills the upper portion of the card.
sections.Symbol: Mounted chivalric force; the cavalier of swords represents swift, decisive action that overwhelms obstacles.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.Scene: A long-bearded elder in a richly decorated green and red robe sits at lower left, facing right. He holds a round lantern-like object in each hand. The scale is intimate, the figure occupying the lower third against an elaborate gold-leaf background.
sections.Symbol: The king of wands embodies mature creative authority; the lanterns or sceptres he holds suggest the illuminating power of the wand suit.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.Scene: A knight in full plate armour rides a white horse to the left. He wears a plumed helmet and carries a tall baton or staff upright in his right hand. The horse's red bridle is decorated with gold roundels; a dark shield is strapped to the rider's back.
sections.Symbol: Mounted creative energy; the cavalier of batons represents enthusiasm, ambition, and the drive to move forward.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.Scene: A standing youth in blue armour holds a tall baton upright in his right hand and faces slightly left. His stance is upright and frontal against a gold damask background. A dark shape — possibly a shield or bag — is partially visible at lower left.
sections.Symbol: The page of batons as messenger of creative potential; youthful energy not yet set in motion.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.Scene: A bearded elder in a crown and richly layered robes sits on a throne. His robes are red and blue with gold details; he holds a large ornamental coin or disk in his left hand, and his right hand rests across his chest. The expression is dignified and authoritative.
sections.Symbol: The king of coins represents mature material authority — the wise steward of resources and physical abundance.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.Scene: A crowned woman in a blue and green gown is seated on a throne. She holds a large, ornate golden chalice in her left hand and a sceptre in her right. The cup is elaborate and cup-shaped, the focal object of the card.
sections.Symbol: The queen of cups embodies emotional depth, intuition, and the rich inner life symbolised by the overflowing vessel.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.About: The mountebank — the first trump (atout I).
sections.Scene: A plumed-hat merchant or conjurer stands at a table on which several objects — cups, coins, and small tools — are arranged. Two smaller figures, one on each side, attend him. The main figure bends over the table in the classic Bagatto pose.
sections.Symbol: Il Bagatto (the Mountebank) represents skill, manual cleverness, and the power of will to manipulate the material world.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.Scene: An enthroned bearded elder in white and red papal robes and a triple tiara raises his right hand in blessing. He carries a crozier or cross-staff. The gilded background and authoritative pose identify him clearly as Il Papa.
sections.Symbol: Spiritual hierarchy and the dispensation of blessing through institutional authority.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.Scene: A winged seated figure — possibly an angel — pours liquid from one vessel to another held close to the body. The figure is crowned and wears a blue gown; the act of pouring is shown mid-motion.
sections.Symbol: Temperance as controlled mixing: the virtue of measured moderation that prevents excess in either direction.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.Scene: Two standing figures in courtly dress face each other beneath a large, prominent eight-pointed gold star against a dark blue-grey sky. The star dominates the upper portion of the card; the figures below appear to gesture toward it or toward each other.
sections.Symbol: The star as a celestial guide; the two figures suggest contemplation of divine guidance or the gift of renewed hope.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.Scene: An elderly robed man sits in a chamber beside a spinning wheel or armillary sphere-like device. A crescent moon is visible above in a darkened sky. The man appears to write, calculate, or contemplate while the moon arcs overhead.
sections.Symbol: The moon governs time, cycles, and the hidden interior life; the scholarly elder suggests deep study of the cyclic and the uncertain.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.Scene: A radiant human-faced sun shines at the top. Below, two figures — a kneeling elder and a standing youth — face each other before an arched architectural portal. The scene suggests a revelation or transmission occurring under the sun's beneficent light.
sections.Symbol: The sun as the source of clarity and vitality, here illuminating a scene of instruction or emergence from shadow.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.Scene: A winged putto or small angel sits atop a large oval wreath of green laurel. Within the wreath, a detailed cityscape is depicted — towers, buildings, water, and perhaps distant hills. Below the wreath, a bird with spread wings (eagle or pelican) faces upward.
sections.Symbol: The world as a protected, enclosed domain presided over by celestial beings. The wreath frames earthly civilisation; the eagle below signifies sovereignty and reach.

metadata.collection: Beinecke Library, Yale (Cary Collection) — PLAYING CARDS GEN 966

sections.About: The Fool / Mat — the unnumbered wanderer.
sections.Scene: A barefoot, near-naked figure in a colourful patchwork costume stands centrally, with a folded cloth or hood visible above his head. Two smaller attendant figures flank him — one appears to nip at his coat or legs. The fool is shown in an animated, comic posture.
sections.Symbol: Il Matto, the unnumbered fool, represents freedom from convention, the spirit before attachment. His ragged costume and comic attendants signal his position outside all rank and structure.

sections.What it is: The eight trumps that survive — Bagatto, Pope, Temperance, Star, Moon, Sun, World and the Fool — out of an original sequence now mostly lost.
sections.Scene: Eight surviving trump cards from the Este Tarot, a set of hand-painted cards produced c. 1450 for the Este court in Ferrara. The surviving trumps are: Il Bagatto, Il Papa, Temperance, The Star, The Moon, The Sun, The World, and Il Matto. The cards are painted on heavy vellum with tempera, gold leaf, and fine brushwork.
sections.Symbol: The Este set represents an early stage of the trionfi tradition, before the iconography had fully stabilised. Its survival as eight disconnected trumps hints at the fragility of the court tarot tradition.

sections.What it is: Eight court cards across the four Italian suits (Swords, Batons, Coins, Cups) — kings, queens, a cavalier and a jack.
sections.Scene: Eight surviving court cards from the Este Tarot: King and Queen of Swords, Cavalier of Swords, King of Batons, Cavalier of Batons, Jack of Batons, King of Coins, and Queen of Cups. Each card depicts a courtly figure in the dress and style of the mid-15th century North Italian court.
sections.Symbol: Court cards in this tradition depict the social ranks of the court: king, queen, cavalier (mounted knight), and jack (page). The Este court cards are among the earliest surviving examples of the suit-court structure that became standard in European playing cards.

image_credit: Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale University (Cary Collection, PLAYING CARDS GEN 966)
